Hyperscaling · The AI Buildout

The Build and the Bill

American tech is spending the better part of a trillion dollars a year building artificial-intelligence infrastructure that AI revenue cannot come close to paying back. So who does pay — and who is left holding the bet when the math comes due? A clear-eyed reckoning, from the arithmetic down to who carries the bill.

By Michael Nolan
Updated July 19, 2026

It is the largest private capital bet in history. In 2025 seven companies committed about $424 billion to building artificial-intelligence infrastructure; for 2026 the five biggest are guiding, between them, toward something like $690 billion — roughly double their 2024 spending — and the buildout will have absorbed on the order of $5.3 trillion of capital by 2030. Set against that is the revenue it earns today: all the AI-specific services in the economy, frontier labs included, run at roughly $80 billion a year. The returns, in other words, are a forecast; the costs are already here. This report follows that asymmetry all the way down — can a bet this size ever be paid back, and if it can’t, who is left holding it, and who is already paying while the forecast plays out?

A decade ago these firms spent on the order of $0 billion a year between them. The total roughly doubled in the single year to 2025, and a single year’s spending now equals close to a third — 31 percent — of everything American business invests in information-technology equipment and software combined. This is no longer a sector story. It is a claim on the economy’s entire investable surplus, made by a handful of boardrooms.

Widen the lens to the whole economy and it reads the same way. Real investment in information-processing equipment and software has climbed from about 2.6 percent of GDP in 2010 to roughly 7.0 percent in 2026 — and in the first half of 2025, the growth in that one line was equal to about 92 percent of all US GDP growth. Strip it out and the economy barely moved. That is a contribution, not a proven cause — absent the boom, cheaper credit or power would have offset part of the gap — but it means headline growth now leans on this single category of spending to a degree with few precedents.

Nor is the bet confined to the familiar handful of hyperscalers. Follow the supply chain outward and it fans across at least seven layers — memory, chip-making equipment, networking, data-center landlords, electrical and cooling gear, power producers, and the neoclouds that rent out GPUs — some 18 US-listed firms carrying on the order of $399 billion of AI-exposed revenue and $45 billion of capital spending. That revenue figure is an upper bound, not an “AI total”: it counts each firm’s whole business wherever the AI slice can’t be cleanly separated, is never summed into one headline number, and it understates memory, where only Micron is US-listed. The point is not the sum but the reach — the buildout has pulled a wide cross-section of the industrial economy into its orbit.

The Arithmetic

Start with the version that looks impossible, because it is the honest starting point. The buildout has committed roughly $5.3 trillion of capital at a cost of capital around 10 percent. To earn that back — even at the 60 percent operating margin Amazon’s AWS earns at scale, the most profitable cloud business ever built — the AI economy would have to generate on the order of $3.5 trillion in revenue every year. Today it generates about $80 billion.

Demand is, to be clear, exploding: Google now processes more than seven times as many AI tokens each year as the last. But run that growth forward on an adoption curve, net the collapsing price per token, and achievable AI revenue reaches roughly $0.73 trillion by 2030 in the central case — $1.4 trillion even in an optimistic one. Against a requirement of $3.5 trillion or more, the curve does not close the gap. It does not come within several times of it.

To pay the buildout back from AI revenue, that revenue would have to grow about 156 percent a year — roughly 4 times AWS’s pace over its thirteen-year climb, at far greater scale. Nothing in the history of business has done it.

So Who Actually Pays?

If the arithmetic is that bad, the buildout should have stopped. It has not, and the reason is the hinge of this whole story: the giants are not paying for it out of AI revenue. They are paying for it out of the monopolies they already own. Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon and Meta together throw off something like $683 billion a year in operating cash flow — from Search ads, Office, Windows, AWS and the ad machines — and across the group that cash still covers their capital spending about 1.7 times over. They are spending Search and advertising profits on AI data centers.

That is why the giants are not in danger of insolvency, whatever happens to AI revenue — their risk is wasted shareholder value, not bankruptcy. But look at the firms below the line. Oracle’s operating cash flow covers only about 0.98× its capital spending; CoreWeave’s, about 0.30×. They cannot pay from earnings, so they pay with debt, equity raises and contracted revenue — and a striking share of that contracted revenue turns out to be the same money going around in a circle.

The Circle

By one estimate, about $800 billion of arrangements now loop capital around a ring. Nvidia is putting up to $100 billion into OpenAI. OpenAI has committed something like $1.4 trillion over the next several years — around $300 billion to Oracle, with hundreds of billions more pledged to Microsoft, Broadcom, AMD and CoreWeave. And those providers, in turn, spend much of it buying Nvidia’s chips. Nvidia books the sales as revenue and takes equity stakes in the very customers placing the orders — roughly 7 percent of CoreWeave among them. Each lap around the ring is counted, somewhere, as growth.

Set OpenAI’s $1.4 trillion of commitments beside its roughly $25 billion of actual annual revenue and the structure gives itself away. This is vendor financing: a supplier funding the customers who buy its product, so that the purchases show up as independent demand. Wall Street has seen the movie before — Lucent and Nortel booked loans to their own customers as equipment sales right up to the 2000 telecom bust, when the customers turned out unable to pay.

It is not pure illusion: real compute is delivered, real chips are installed, and the demand for tokens underneath is genuine. But the ring flatters apparent demand and concentrates the danger, because it runs on one input no balance sheet controls — fresh capital continuing to flow in. The day investors stop funding the next OpenAI round is the day Oracle’s contracted revenue, CoreWeave’s debt service and Nvidia’s order book all wobble at once. The circle is a confidence machine; it spins beautifully until it doesn’t.

And there is a sharper version of that fragility — the one that actually ended the last credit cycle. The ring does not need investors to stop funding the next round; it only needs them to slow down. Subprime mortgages in 2008 were written to be refinanced before their teaser rates reset, and they detonated not when house prices fell but when prices merely stopped rising faster — the acceleration going into reverse while the level was still near its peak. The AI circle has the same shape: the labs at its center service their commitments by raising each round at a higher mark than the last, so a decelerating valuation, not a falling one, is enough to seize the machinery. And the capital those marks run on is not free-standing AI enthusiasm; it is the same cheap, yield-seeking credit that has bid up every asset for a decade, some of it borrowed abroad at near-zero rates. That makes the trip-wire monetary as much as technological: a central bank tightening, or that cheap-funding trade unwinding, would slow the marks on its own — no failure of AI required.

They Know

The executives running these firms are not fooled by the arithmetic above; they can do the sums better than anyone. What looks irrational from outside is the rational move from inside, and their own stated logic is blunt: the risk of under-investing is greater than the risk of over-investing. Three things make that true for them even if the aggregate is a bubble.

First, it is defensive insurance on a far larger franchise. If AI becomes the way people get answers, Google’s search-ad business — the thing that actually pays for all this — is the thing most at risk; spending a hundred billion to defend a three-hundred-billion-a-year monopoly is rational at almost any ROI. Second, it is a winner-take-most option: if transformative AI has even a modest chance of being enormous, the expected value justifies enormous spending. Third, it is a trap of the prisoner’s-dilemma kind — each firm builds because the others are building, and none can afford to be the one that sat out if AI turns out to matter. That is exactly how individually-rational actors manufacture a collective bubble.

And underneath the defensiveness is a genuine bull case. If AI really does become the substrate of knowledge work, its “revenue” stops being a metered-token line item and becomes a cut of the labor it replaces — a slice of GDP measured in trillions. That is the world the spending implies. Run the payback as a probability and the split is stark: the buildout clears its cost of capital in only about 7 percent of outcomes if it must be repaid by AI-specific revenue, but about 48 percent if AI is credited with lifting all of cloud, advertising and search. The entire bet hinges on which of those worlds arrives — and even the generous one is a coin flip.

The Bill

Here the asymmetry from the start of this piece turns concrete. The payback is a forecast; the running costs are not a forecast at all. They are already being paid — and increasingly by the public. The first is electricity. US power demand was flat for thirteen years; it is now rising about 1.8 percent a year, with data centers a principal cause, climbing from 4.4 percent of US electricity in 2023 toward 9 and 17 percent by 2030.

By 2026 power, not money or chips, had become the binding constraint: roughly 50 percent of planned US data centers are delayed or canceled, and Microsoft is sitting on something like $80 billion of orders it cannot fill because it cannot find the electricity to run chips already in its warehouses. Nor will efficiency rescue the grid — it accelerates the strain. Chips get about 24 percent more efficient each year, but to meet the projected energy path compute must grow 40–57 percent a year, roughly twice as fast. Efficiency spared the grid perhaps 1,084 terawatt-hours by 2028 — and demand still tripled. It is the oldest result in energy economics, the Jevons paradox, playing out in real time.

The pattern repeats inside the machine. Decompose the full cost of a deployed AI accelerator and only about 8 percent is the electricity to run it; some 24 percent is the building and cooling; and fully 60 percent is Nvidia’s gross margin — scarcity rent, not the cost of making anything. In the data-center-heavy states, residential power has risen about 38 percent in five years against 28 percent elsewhere; whether the data centers caused it is genuinely contested, but the mechanism, where it operates, is plain. The margin, in other words, is privatised, and the bill is increasingly socialised.

The margin is private. The bill — the grid, the prices, the water — is public.

Is the Demand Even There?

All of which assumes the revenue eventually shows up. The early evidence is mixed, and the most-hyped version of it is receding. A widely cited MIT study found that about 95 percent of enterprise generative-AI pilots delivered no measurable return; by one survey 29 percent of firms that cut staff for AI have rehired, and 55 percent of employers regret the cuts. Usage in tokens is vertical; usage in dollars, and in delivered productivity, lags well behind it.

The gap between what AI can do and what it is paid to do is the adoption runway still ahead — and the buildout’s entire case is that the runway gets crossed, at a high margin, before the financing runs out. That remains a wager on a future, not a description of the present.

What “Popping” Would Mean

So suppose the wager sours — or merely stops accelerating fast enough to fund the next round. “The AI bubble bursting” conjures the year 2000, but the analogy misleads, because the people who actually owe the money are not the ones who look most exposed. Popping would not mean AI is exposed as fake. It would mean the leveraged claims stacked on top of a real-but-overbuilt infrastructure repricing to what it truly earns — and that repricing lands on the fringe, not the giants. The Oracles, CoreWeaves and labs hit refinancing walls; Nvidia’s valuation multiple compresses; and depreciation finally catches up — the short-seller Michael Burry argues an AI server’s true working life is closer to two or three years — about 2.5 years at the midpoint — well under the six years the books assume, which would gut reported profits if applied.

Because the bet has been indexed into everyone’s savings — seven names are now about 35 percent of the S&P 500, and the premium for holding stocks over safe bonds has thinned to roughly 0.5 percent — that repricing would not stay contained to a few balance sheets; it would run straight through the index funds in ordinary retirement accounts. And then the part that rhymes with history: the assets persist. The dark fiber of 1999 was eventually all lit. The data centers and chips will not vanish; they will be repriced, absorbed and, in time, used — while Microsoft keeps printing Office money throughout.

Does the Math Math?

As a standalone investment — AI revenue paying back AI capital — no. It clears its cost of capital in roughly 7 percent of plausible paths, on the most generous mature-cloud margins, and the revenue ramp it needs has no precedent. As something else — spend the franchise’s monopoly profits on a defensive option, push the fragile financing onto whoever is furthest out on the limb, and let the public carry the grid and the prices — it pencils out fine, for the giants. Both can be true at once: a genuine technological revolution and a speculative mania. They almost always have been. Canals, railways, electricity, the internet — each delivered, and each ruined a generation of financiers first.

The distributional ledger is the starkest form of the same asymmetry. Stock-market wealth in America is not widely held: the top tenth of households own about 93 percent of all equities and the bottom half roughly one percent (Federal Reserve, 2024), and over the past quarter-century the net worth of the top 1 percent has risen from about $10 trillion to more than $50 trillion (Fed Distributional Financial Accounts). So the upside of the buildout — the seven stocks, the concentrated gains — accrues to a narrow band that already owns the market, while its costs, from the power bill to a repricing that would run through every indexed retirement account, are borne broadly. The heterodox economists Michael Hudson and Radhika Desai read the whole episode this way, as a credit-fuelled bubble whose rewards and risks fall on different people; one need not share their forecast of an imminent depression to accept the incidence.

The honest verdict is therefore not a forecast but an accounting, and it comes down to the asymmetry this report has tracked from its first lines. The returns are a forecast; the costs are already here — and the two do not fall on the same people. The profits concentrate at a handful of chokepoints — Nvidia’s margin, a single Taiwanese foundry, a few cloud balance sheets. The costs spread outward: onto ratepayers and the grid, onto the leveraged firms at the edge of the circle, and onto everyone whose retirement is indexed to the same seven stocks. The companies know the arithmetic does not close on AI revenue alone, and they spend anyway, because for each of them it is the rational move — which is precisely how the overhang gets built. So the question was never whether artificial intelligence is real. It is real. The question is who is left holding the bet when its claims finally reprice to what the machines can actually earn — and, long before that reckoning, who is paying for it now.

How this was measured

The spine of this report is a reproducible, primary-source pipeline. Hyperscaler and chip-maker capital spending, debt, depreciation and margins are extracted from SEC EDGAR filings, and the wider supply-chain footprint from EDGAR company facts; macro, rates and credit series from the Federal Reserve’s FRED; national-accounts investment and output, and household wealth distribution, from the Bureau of Economic Analysis and the Fed’s Distributional Financial Accounts; electricity demand and prices, national and by state, from the US Energy Information Administration; occupational employment from the Bureau of Labor Statistics; accelerator specifications from vendor datasheets cross-checked against Epoch AI. Data-center energy is from Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory; AI-exposure scores from Eloundou et al. and the Anthropic Economic Index; price modelling from the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.

The chip total-cost-of-ownership, economic-obsolescence, token-economics and Jevons models are the author’s, built on those inputs; vendor performance figures, bill-of-materials estimates and the 2026 capex/revenue and adoption figures are second-tier (analyst and press) and are flagged as such, attributed in the text, and listed in Sources below. They frame; they are not the data of record. Contested questions — whether data centers cause local price rises, whether the buildout is “backstopped,” what an AI chip’s true life is — are left contested. Full dataset-by-dataset provenance is in notes/provenance.md in the project repository.

Sources & further reading

Framing figures cited above are journalism and analyst estimates, not the primary-source pipeline; they are attributed inline and flagged Tier-2. They move quarter to quarter — treat them as of mid-2026 and confirm against the linked source before re-quoting. Full dataset-by-dataset provenance, including these URLs, is in notes/provenance.md.

Adoption & the hype reversal. MIT NANDA, “The GenAI Divide” (95% of enterprise pilots show no P&L return), via Fortune, Aug 2025; Robert Half and Forrester on post-AI re-hiring, via the Washington Times, Mar 2026.

Capex, revenue & circular financing. 2026 hyperscaler capex guidance (Futurum, MUFG) and OpenAI’s ~$1.4 trillion in commitments; the ~$800 billion vendor-financing loop, via BlockEden, Mar 2026; 2026 data-center delays, via Tech Insider; Microsoft’s unfilled, unpowered Azure orders, via Global Data Center Hub.

GPU depreciation & useful life. Michael Burry (posting as “Cassandra Unchained”) argues hyperscalers flatter earnings by booking a 5–6-year life on hardware whose real economic life is ~2–3 years — an estimated ~$176 billion of understated depreciation across the sector for 2026–2028 — in posts on X (Nov 2025) and his Substack, reported by CNBC (Nov 11, 2025). Burry disclosed put positions on Nvidia and Palantir. For the counter-view — that retired training GPUs find a long second life in inference — see SiliconANGLE (Nov 2025).

Enron or telecom — which analogy? The depreciation critique is often wrapped in the Enron story: Jim Chanos, who called Enron by reading the footnote everyone skipped, now reads the useful-life footnote the same way, and adds a seller-side twist — the same buildout dollar is booked as high-margin revenue by the chip-maker now while the buyer expenses it slowly over six years, so index-level earnings flatter the underlying economics until the spending stops. The mechanic is real, but the Enron label overreaches. Enron was fraud — debt hidden in off-balance-sheet vehicles, self-dealing, fabricated marks; hyperscaler useful-life extensions are disclosed judgments sitting in the 10-K, aggressive but legal, at firms that generate real cash. The tighter historical rhyme is the one Chanos himself reaches for when he calls Nvidia’s investing-in-its-own-customers “the most sophisticated vendor-financing scheme since Lucent” — the 2000 telecom overbuild: genuine demand overshoot, double- and triple-ordering of scarce gear, then cancellation, ending in Cisco’s ~$2.2 billion inventory writedown in 2001. That is an overinvestment failure, not a concealment one — and it is the more apt, and more sobering, template for an AI capex cycle whose returns are still a forecast. (Chanos’s sharpest single data point: feeding CoreWeave’s own ~2–3-year GPU-life estimate into the model implies roughly 0% return on invested capital.)

The financing-cycle reading. Two outside analyses reach this report’s conclusion by different routes. An independent essay, “The Second Derivative” (Groundbrkr, 2026), argues the AI boom is financed like the 2008 credit cycle rather than the 2000 tech cycle, and that a mere deceleration in capex and funding — not a reversal — is enough to break structures built for perpetual acceleration. Economists Michael Hudson and Radhika Desai, in Geopolitical Economy Hour #80 (Jul 2026), read the same buildout as a credit-fuelled “Ponzi capital-gains bubble” led by seven unprofitable AI names and floated on cheap global carry — concentrating the gains in a few hands while socialising the cost. Both are polemical, prediction-forward framings, not primary data; this report borrows their mechanism (a rollover dependency a monetary slowdown can trip) while declining their timing calls.
